title: The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.
caption:
The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita

The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.

This scatter plot maps countries based on their GDP per capita against their disease burden, measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 people. The horizontal axis represents GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, plotted on a logarithmic scale ranging from roughly $2,000 to over $100,000. The vertical axis displays DALYs, spanning from 0 to 80,000. A distinct negative correlation is visible across the dataset: nations with lower GDP per capita generally exhibit higher disease burdens. Countries in Africa, colored purple, such as the Central African Republic, Niger, and Chad, cluster in the upper-left quadrant, showing DALY rates between 60,000 and 80,000 alongside GDP figures below $5,000. Conversely, high-income nations in North America and Europe, represented by orange and blue markers respectively, occupy the bottom-right section. The United States, Germany, and Japan display GDP per capita figures exceeding $40,000 while maintaining DALY rates below 30,000. Circle sizes correspond to population, highlighting large nations like India and China in the middle of the distribution; India sits near $10,000 GDP with approximately 35,000 DALYs, while China is positioned near $20,000 GDP with roughly 20,000 DALYs. Intermediate economies like Brazil and Indonesia bridge the gap between these extremes, demonstrating a gradual decline in disease burden as economic output increases.
